narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 51% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold that leads to rejection. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, family background, and planned study schedule, focusing on patterns seen in past students without considering gender or racial background. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 class decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the 6th decile average among those who passed, signaling to the model a potential struggle in later undergraduate performance that often correlates with bar exam challenges. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.70, well above the 3.24 average for passers, which pulled the prediction toward success by highlighting your overall academic capability. Your LSAT score of 34 also helped in that direction, though it fell short of the 37.27 average for those who passed, providing moderate reassurance of your aptitude. Other elements, like your lower-middle family income bracket—less common among bar passers who are more often from middle or upper-middle backgrounds—and your plan to attend part-time, which only 6.8% of successful students did, played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers. Compared to typical accepted students, who tend to have higher decile rankings in later years, stronger LSATs, and full-time focus, your profile showed promise in GPA but was held back by that key Year 3 dip and other mismatches. This close call at 51% means small improvements could shift future outcomes dramatically. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward 37 or higher, strengthening your later-year grades if possible through additional coursework, and exploring full-time options or income-support resources to align more with successful profiles. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting these areas—reach out to advisors for personalized prep plans, and reapply once you've addressed the biggest hurdles. Your solid undergrad record gives you a strong foundation to build on.
